---
subject: "What I’ve been reading"
from: "Marginal REVOLUTION <donotreply@wordpress.com>"
to: ""
date: 2025-02-27 06:06:22
labels: ["CATEGORY_PERSONAL", "INBOX", "Tyler Cowen", "UNREAD"]
label_ids: ["CATEGORY_PERSONAL", "INBOX", "Label_2466199532210586699", "UNREAD"]
---
Alain Mabanckou, [Dealing with the Dead](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=00753e08eb0dc696a04043cb778441a0b94ee5626f56571d46a29b3514eb7ac7&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=1c7cb6bb886602b9fa56f2cff7de235c&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). Most African fiction does not connect with me, and there is a tendency for the reviews to be untrustworthy. This "cemetery memoir," from the Congo (via UCLA), connected with me and held my interest throughout.
Philip Zaleski and Carol Zaleski, [The Fellowship: The Literary Lives of the Inklings: J.R.R. Tolkien, C.S. Lewis, Owen Barfield, Charles Williams](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=ce264fa5402720ea4a466368d873900c49e34441607b1dbb5b77efae8fb9cd2a&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=9416fa777c2c1b91dc1154f73e919a92&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). I was in the mood of thinking I don't need to read another book about these people. Yet this one was so good it won me over nonetheless.
Eddie Huffman, [Doc Watson: A Life in Music](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=c509554e2b44bf7c0daf62e38a71c419c078ab3fc203a636e0838282cab5d934&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=c7f21b273388dab46e3b22398570124a&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). A fun book about one of America's [greatest guitarists](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=09452a19404253276ec3c4a1354facd1d324a8be6023d2ef7a4b14d0c9c4328d&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=840dadb9ddf23714e3fc0ad2de3f288f&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_url=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cueW91dHViZS5jb20vd2F0Y2g/dj02VkFicm5qZHRZdw=). Watson was blind from an early age, and he was collecting state disability benefits until he was 40 -- a classic late bloomer.
Philip Freeman, [In the Brewing Luminous: The Life and Music of Cecil Taylor](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=e6487a7b8175b8d86830603601dce78b2bc2cf762b1c5bf359df793fefc2a0d8&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=2670bdd29bf7e48492826b0cfd77da75&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). Call me crazy, but I think Sun Ra and Taylor are better and more important musically than say Duke Ellington. Freeman's book is the first full-length biography of Taylor, and it is well-informed and properly appreciative. It induced me to buy another book by him. The evening I saw Taylor was one of the greatest of my life, I thank my mother for coming with me.
Carlos M.N. Eire, [They Flew: A History of the Impossible](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=413945829a5a76152bb0269cc5d726720fc4716a318e966fb2c94b333eed9177&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=747ea2b0208ed24c95bffcbbb728bd4e&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). Ross Douthat recommended this one to me. It is well done, and worth reading, but I don't find it shifted my priors on whether "impossible" events might have really happened.
I agree with the central arguments of Samir Varma's [The Science of Free Will: How Determinism Affects Everything from the Future of AI to Traffic to God to Bees](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=9478462331a347a346ab353fa974c8a3b91e461209e96ed1c80ef4b231ec83b1&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=58d6ef745f8e97b15abd34075de3a6e6&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_url=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYW1hem9uLmNvbS9TY2llbmNlLUZyZWUtV2lsbC1EZXRlcm1pbmlzbS1FdmVyeXRoaW5nL2RwLzE4MDM0MTczMTU). I was happy to write a foreword for the book.
Kathleen deLaski, [Who Needs College Anymore? Imagining a Future Where Degrees Don't Matter](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=01eedec92a01dc61e8d876809c0ab83a7632bf965c9fc48184eabc7b82360d76&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=3e850ffd09bf45d17de8cc7c36a21679&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_url=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYW1hem9uLmNvbS9XaG8tTmVlZHMtQ29sbGVnZS1Bbnltb3JlLUltYWdpbmluZy9kcC8xNjgyNTM5NTIw). One of a growing chorus of books suggesting higher education is on the verge of some radical changes.
There is Daniel Brook, [The Einstein of Sex: Dr. Magnus Hirschfeld Visionary of Weimar Berlin](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=add3c54f2be03e8c8ce39a920da98465bcd2d2cd6d19a8fa88033c26ee750a4d&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=362eb3c31c5994f6b834e72c1f089e24&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_). It is good to see him getting more attention.
There is also Brandy Schillace, [The Intermediaries: A Weimar Story](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=c06fc9bcfdc61da4eacdabcd2948721abfa9d075d80cf2fc23502a7c5bcafb7c&blog_id=42693868&post_id=90056&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=a3e4a22bef12e52ffcadbfa1d525986d&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_).
